返回本批次

在 AWS 工作了 20 年，但从来都不是我的工作

20 years on AWS and never not my job

www.daemonology.net 作者 cperciva 分数 243 评论 62 2026/04/11 13:31
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 我于 2006 年 4 月 10 日晚上 10:31 创建了我的第一个 AWS 帐户。我看到了 Amazon S3 的公告，并且一直在模糊地思考安全备份的问题 - 尽管我直到几个月后才启动 Tarsnap - 在线存储服务的想法吸引了我。事实上，它是一项网络服务，这使它更具吸引力。自 1998 年以来，我一直在构建 Web 服务，当时我决定通过 HTTP 协调创世界纪录的 Pi 计算比通过电子邮件进行更容易。
  2. 虽然我因为对 Amazon S3 感兴趣而创建了 AWS 账户，但实际上我并不能立即使用该账户：在 AWS 的早期，您必须专门要求为您的账户启用每项新服务。不过，我的新 AWS 账户确实默认启用了两项服务：Amazon Simple Queue Service（大多数人都知道它是“第一个 AWS 服务”）和 Amazon E-Commerce Service（一个 API，它允许 Amazon 附属机构访问 Amazon.com 的产品目录）——这是真正的第一个 AWS 服务，但大多数人从未听说过，并且已从 AWS 历史中悄然消失。
  3. 没过多久我就开始抱怨事情。此时我是 FreeBSD 安全官，因此我对云中任何事物的第一个兴趣是安全性。 AWS 请求使用 API 密钥进行签名，提供身份验证和完整性保护 - 不仅确认用户已获得授权，而且确认请求未被篡改。然而，AWS 响应上没有相应的签名 - 此时通过 HTTP 而不是 HTTPS 发出 AWS 请求仍然很常见，因此响应篡改的可能性非常真实。我不记得当我在（早已消失的）AWS 开发者论坛上发布此内容时，是否有来自 Amazon 的人表现出任何兴趣，但我仍然认为这将是一件好事：随着请求通过 TLS，它现在显然不再那么重要，但端到端签名总是比传输层安全性更好。
  4. 当然，Amazon EC2 推出后我就有了一个新目标：我想在上面运行 FreeBSD！我通过杰夫·巴尔 (Jeff Barr) 的博客联系了他，他让我与亚马逊内部的人员取得了联系，2007 年初，我获得了第一份亚马逊 NDA。 （有趣的故事，2007 年亚马逊仍在使用传真机，但我没有传真机，所以当我将湿墨水签名邮寄到西雅图时，我的第一次简报被推迟了。）我听到的简报功能之一是“自定义内核”；与今天 AWS Lambda 的工作方式非常相似，Amazon EC2 推出时没有任何“自带内核”支持。显然，为了给 EC2 带来 FreeBSD 支持，我需要使用这个功能，它于 2007 年 11 月推出，当时 Amazon EC2 获得了运行 Red Hat 的能力；该公告发出后不久，我的 FreeBSD 帐户就被列入内部“发布 Amazon 内核映像”API 的许可名单。
  5. 但我没有等到这个功能提供，就提供了有关 Amazon EC2 的更多反馈。 2007 年 3 月，我向一位亚马逊人表达了对 Xen 安全性的担忧——当时它还是一个相当新的系统，亚马逊是第一个在真正恶劣的环境中部署它的系统——并鼓励他们雇人对代码进行彻底的安全审计。当与我交谈的亚马逊人承认他们不知道该与谁合作时，我想到了我在担任 FreeBSD 安全官期间共事过的人，并向他们推荐了 Tavis Ormandy。同年晚些时候，Tavis 被认为报告了 Xen 中的两个漏洞（CVE-2007-1320 和 CVE-2007-1321）；这些事件之间是否有任何联系，我不知道。

Original Summary

  1. I created my first AWS account at 10:31 PM on April 10th, 2006. I had seen the announcement of Amazon S3 and had been thinking vaguely about the problem of secure backups — even though I didn't start Tarsnap until several months later — and the idea of an online storage service appealed to me. The fact that it was a web service made it even more appealing; I had been building web services since 1998, when I decided that coordinating a world-record-setting computation of Pi over HTTP would be easier than doing it over email.
  2. While I created my AWS account because I was interested in Amazon S3, that was not in fact immediately available to me: In the early days of AWS, you had to specifically ask for each new service to be enabled for your account. My new AWS account did come with two services enabled by default, though — Amazon Simple Queue Service, which most people know as "the first AWS service", and Amazon E-Commerce Service, an API which allowed Amazon affiliates to access Amazon.com's product catalogue — which was the real first AWS service, but which most people have never heard of and which has been quietly scrubbed from AWS history.
  3. It didn't take long before I started complaining about things. By this point I was the FreeBSD Security Officer, so my first interest with anything in the cloud was security. AWS requests are signed with API keys providing both authentication and integrity protection — confirming not only that the user was authorized, but also that the request hadn't been tampered with. There is, however, no corresponding signature on AWS responses — and at this time it was still very common to make AWS requests over HTTP rather than HTTPS, so the possibility of response tampering was very real. I don't recall if anyone from Amazon showed any interest when I posted about this on the (long-disappeared) AWS Developer Forums, but I still think it would be a good thing to have: With requests going over TLS it is obviously less critical now, but end-to-end signing is always going to be better than transport-layer security.
  4. Of course, as soon as Amazon EC2 launched I had a new target: I wanted to run FreeBSD on it! I reached out to Jeff Barr via his blog and he put me in touch with people inside Amazon, and in early 2007 I had my first Amazon NDA. (Funny story, in 2007 Amazon was still using fax machines — but I didn't have a fax machine, so my first briefing was delayed while I snail-mailed a wet-ink signature down to Seattle.) Among the features I was briefed on was "Custom Kernels"; much like how AWS Lambda works today, Amazon EC2 launched without any "bring your own kernel" support. Obviously, to bring FreeBSD support to EC2 I was going to need to use this functionality, and it launched in November 2007 when Amazon EC2 gained the ability to run Red Hat; soon after that announcement went out, my FreeBSD account was allowlisted for the internal "publish Amazon Kernel Images" API.
  5. But I didn't wait for this functionality to be offered before providing more feedback about Amazon EC2. In March 2007 I expressed concerns to an Amazonian about the security of Xen — it was at the time still quite a new system and Amazon was the first to be deploying it in truly hostile environments — and encouraged them to hire someone to do a thorough security audit of the code. When the Amazonian I was speaking to admitted that they didn't know who to engage for this, I thought about the people I had worked with in my time as FreeBSD Security Officer and recommended Tavis Ormandy to them. Later that year, Tavis was credited with reporting two vulnerabilities in Xen (CVE-2007-1320 and CVE-2007-1321); whether there is any connection between those events, I do not know.

评论（默认中文）

gobdovan 2026/04/11 15:02
作者称英雄只是无薪的亚马逊员工为“笑话”，但现实不会因为好笑而成为笑话。这里的不对称性是惊人的。我发现自己阻碍了私人研究，因为我不想为已经足够高效的价值提取机器提供免费研发。

作者的贡献至少是依赖驱动的，但在这种依赖之外，当关系如此单方面时，即使“公开”也很难证明贡献是合理的。尤其是亚马逊，对宽容的开源曾经依赖的经济假设造成了巨大的损害。越来越多的项目采用“商业源码许可证”，正是为了防止开放工作成为超大规模货币化的免费投入。

这些开发人员知道亚马逊是贪婪的，在某种程度上，他们的社区贡献上游的唯一主要成果是为一个价值数万亿美元的实体提供无偿劳动力，该实体还通过将用户引导到同一软件的托管版本来转移对原始项目的支持和社区参与。

The author calls it a 'joke' that Heroes are just unpaid Amazon employees, but reality doesn't become a joke just because it's funny. The asymmetry here is staggering. I find myself holding back private research because I don't want to provide free R&D for a value-extraction machine that is already efficient enough.

The author was at least dependency-driven in their contribution, but outside that kind of dependency, it's hard to justify contributing even 'in the open' when the relationship is this one-sided. Amazon in particular has done enormous damage to the economic assumptions that permissive open source once relied on. There's increasingly more projects adopting 'Business Source Licenses', precisely to prevent open work from becoming a free input into hyperscaler monetization.

These devs know Amazon is grabby and, at some point, the only dominant outcome their community contribution is upstream of is unpaid labor for a trillion-dollar entity that also diverts support and community engagement away from the original projects by funneling users into managed versions of the same software.

在 HN 中查看
djoldman 2026/04/11 20:43
如果有人不喜欢亚马逊使用他们编写的软件，他们可以直接禁止亚马逊在文案许可证中使用它。

这是完全合法的：“除了亚马逊[以及任何人]，任何人都可以将其用于任何目的，只要……”

亚马逊不会故意使用该软件。不值得承担潜在的法律责任。

这并不意味着亚马逊不会编写自己的版本，但如果他们认为在某个时候需要的话。

If someone doesn't like Amazon using software they write, they can just outright disallow Amazon from using it in the copywrite license.

It's perfectly legal to say: "except for Amazon [and whoever], anyone can use this for any purpose, provided..."

Amazon won't intentionally use that software. It's not worth the potential legal liability.

That doesn't mean Amazon won't write their own version though if they think they need to at some point.

在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/11 21:14
我想说的是，这正是正在发生的事情，但用的是更强有力的语言。如果您不允许亚马逊，则可能有第三方向亚马逊提供我们的服务。所以亚马逊绳子并不是可怕的东西；而是。令人担忧的是他们可以访问的转售或托管服务安排。

因此，您会看到针对基础设施转售而非特定实体的表述，例如：

“为避免疑义，许可证不允许出现以下情况：

* 托管服务允许第三方开发人员...注册他们自己的 [SERVICE] 服务端点并通过该托管服务调用它们。”

“您不得将软件作为托管或托管服务提供给第三方，因为该服务使用户可以访问该软件的任何实质性特性或功能。”

“如果您将程序的功能或修改版本作为服务提供给第三方，则必须根据本许可证的条款，通过网络下载向所有人免费提供服务源代码 [...]，其中“服务源代码”的定义广泛，包括整个托管堆栈（监控、备份等），以确保公平的竞争环境”

I am saying this is exactly what's happening, but with more robust language. If you disallow Amazon, maybe there is a third party that offers our services to Amazon. So Amazon-the-string is not the bogeyman; the concern is the resale or hosted-service arrangement they can access.

So you see formulations that target infrastructure resale rather than specific entities, such as:

"For the avoidance of doubt, the following scenarios are not permitted under the license:

* A managed service that lets third party developers ... register their own [SERVICE] service endpoints and invoke them through that managed service."

"You may not provide the software to third parties as a hosted or managed service, where the service provides users with access to any substantial set of the features or functionality of the software."

"If you make the functionality of the Program or a modified version available to third parties as a service, you must make the Service Source Code available via network download to everyone at no charge, under the terms of this License [...] where 'Service Source Code' is defined broadly to include the entire hosting stack (monitoring, backups, etc.) to ensure a level playing field"

在 HN 中查看
djoldman 2026/04/11 23:33
> 我发现自己阻碍了私人研究，因为我不想为已经足够高效的价值提取机器提供免费研发。

如果有人想以一种公开可见但限制其使用的方式发布技术，他们可以这样做。

如果他们不想发布，则不必发布。

此外，公开发布的技术会破坏可专利性（如果这是目标的话）。

我不明白人们想要实现什么在这里无法实现。

> I find myself holding back private research because I don't want to provide free R&D for a value-extraction machine that is already efficient enough.

If someone wants to release technology in a way that makes it publicly viewable but restricts its use, they can do that.

If they don't want to release it, they don't have to.

Additionally, publicly released technology destroys patentability, if that's the objective.

I don't understand what one would want to achieve that can't be achieved here.

在 HN 中查看
Cpoll 2026/04/11 22:23
> 如果您不允许亚马逊，则可能有第三方向亚马逊提供我们的服务。所以亚马逊绳子并不是可怕的东西；而是。关注的是他们可以访问的转售或托管服务安排

我怀疑亚马逊不会参与这些杂技，因为与客户沟通您的 FooBarDB 在 AWS 中管理但由第三方托管是很尴尬的。

亚马逊将很乐意用他们的后端重新实现您的 API ，就像他们以前所做的那样。

> If you disallow Amazon, maybe there is a third party that offers our services to Amazon. So Amazon-the-string is not the bogeyman; the concern is the resale or hosted-service arrangement they can access

That's some acrobatics I suspect Amazon won't engage in, because communicating to the customer that your FooBarDB is managed in AWS but hosted by a third party is awkward.

Amazon will happily reimplement your API with their backend, as they've done before.

在 HN 中查看
drzaiusx11 2026/04/12 04:23
AFAICT，大型 SaaS 参与者可以简单地实现软件接口，而不管业务源许可证如何，就像 Redis 发生的情况一样，不是吗？或者是否有一些我不知道的针对 API 表面的特定保护措施。我依稀记得谷歌诉甲骨文几乎建立了一些保护措施，但在后来的裁决中被推迟。不过我的记忆已经很模糊了……
AFAICT, large saas players can simply implement the software interfaces regardless of business source licenses like what happened to redis, no? Or is there some specific protections for API surfaces that I'm not aware of. I vaguely recall Google v Oracle almost established some protections but then got deferred in later ruling. My memory is hazy on that though...
在 HN 中查看
ryandvm 2026/04/12 08:14
的确。对于前沿人工智能模型来说，情况比这更糟糕。您实际上可以让他们为您想要克隆的产品编写测试用例，然后对其进行松散的逆向工程代码库。

也就是说，所有这些模型大概都是在开源代码库上训练的，所以看看人工智能黑盒逆向工程是否真的在法庭上站得住脚将会很有趣。

Indeed. And with the frontier AI models it's worse than that. You can literally just have them write test cases for the product you want to clone, then set it loose reverse engineering the code base.

That said, all these models are trained on the open source code bases presumably, so it would be interesting to see if AI-blackbox reverse engineering actually holds up in court.

在 HN 中查看
drzaiusx11 2026/04/12 11:00
我的直觉告诉我，它实际上会在当前的美国法院中成立，但这只是因为大多数公司希望如此，而法院已经对他们有利。

我个人认为不应该，并且人工智能代码不应该被视为“洁净室”方法。也就是说，IANAL。

My gut says it would infact hold up in current US courts, but only because the lionshare of corporations want it to and the courts have been stacked in their favor.

I personally believe it should not and that AI code should NOT be considered a "clean room" method. That said, IANAL.

在 HN 中查看
surgical_fire 2026/04/11 16:50
> 越来越多的项目采用“商业源代码许可”，正是为了防止开放工作成为超大规模货币化的免费投入。

他们可以使用 AGPL 或 GPL3，通常这些许可证在超大规模企业中是被禁止的。

事实是，选择 BSL 的公司从来没有真正想做 OSS，实际上只是为了它的外观，为了在开发人员中获得商誉等。

> There's increasingly more projects adopting 'Business Source Licenses', precisely to prevent open work from becoming a free input into hyperscaler monetization.

They could use AGPL or GPL3, typically those licenses are verboten in hyperscalers.

The truth is that the sort of company opting for BSL never really wanted to do OSS, and in truth only did so for the optics of it, for the goodwill it buys among developers, etc.

在 HN 中查看
noosphr 2026/04/11 18:58
GPL3 可以放在服务器后面，没有人会看到源代码，因为永远不会有任何分发。

只有 AGPL 距离迫使超标量发布他们提供的源代码还很遥远。

The GPL3 can be put behind a server and no one will ever see the source code because there is never any distribution.

Only the AGPL is remotely close to forcing hyper-scalars to release the source code of what they provide.

在 HN 中查看
graemep 2026/04/11 17:45
我知道 AGPL 是这样，但是 GPL3 呢？我认为反对 GPL3 的人是那些向用户分发软件的人（例如，这是 Apple 从 bash 转向 zsh 的原因）。我想不出 GPL3 中有任何内容会对超大规模扩展者造成问题。
I know this is true of AGPL, but GPL3? I thought the people who objected to GPL3 were those distributing software to their users (e.g. was a reason Apple switched from bash to zsh). I cannot think of aything in GPL3 that would be a problem for hyper-scalers.
在 HN 中查看
wasmainiac 2026/04/11 18:01
> 他们可以使用 AGPL 或 GPL3，通常这些许可证在超大规模企业中是被禁止的。

法律的好坏取决于其执行情况，至少在商业领域是这样。不幸的是，我亲眼目睹，如果不被抓到，没有人会关心许可。

企业许可证很好，因为您可以提供支持和其他福利来鼓励付款。

> They could use AGPL or GPL3, typically those licenses are verboten in hyperscalers.

Laws are only as good as their enforcement, in business at least. Unfortunately I have seen first hand that no one cares about licensing if they can’t get caught.

Businesses licenses are good because you can offer support and other benefits to encourage payment.

在 HN 中查看
cxr 2026/04/11 20:43
> 法律的有效性取决于其执行力

据称，这些许可证在这些公司内部被视为无接触——是公司本身坚持要求软件和他们的业务不混合，例如。 Apple 继续发布 Bash 等旧版本的 GNU 程序，然后最终转向 zsh，而不是提供 GPLv3 的更新版本。

GPLv3 和 AGPLv3 都没有提及企业无法使用该软件。

> Laws are only as good as their enforcement

The claim is that those licenses are deemed no-touch within those companies—it's the companies themselves that insist on the software and their business not mixing, e.g. Apple continuing to ship old versions of GNU programs like Bash and then eventually moving to zsh rather than provide updated versions that are GPLv3.

Neither GPLv3 nor AGPLv3 say anything about businesses not being able to use the software.

在 HN 中查看
surgical_fire 2026/04/11 19:46
嘿，闭源、BSL 等都没有问题。我对此很满意。我是最后一个会说有人应该免费赠送他们的作品的人。

我反对的是公司发布具有宽松许可证的软件，然后遭到其他人从中获利的批评，或者在社区采用并为其做出贡献后试图取消宽松许可证。

如果你想玩OSS游戏，那就玩吧。

Hey, nothing wrong with closed source, BSL, etc. I am fine with it. I am the last person that will say someone should give out their work for free.

What I object to is companies releasing software with permissive licenses, and then getting butthurt that others profit from it, or trying to rug pull the permissive licenses after a community adopted and contributed to it.

If you want to play the OSS game, then play it right.

在 HN 中查看
direwolf20 2026/04/11 17:19
或者 SSPL，它扩展了 AGPL，并提供了更多共享要求。
Or SSPL, which extends AGPL with even more sharing requirements.
在 HN 中查看
aleph_minus_one 2026/04/11 19:00
SSPL 不是开源许可证。
The SSPL is not an open-source license.
在 HN 中查看
cxr 2026/04/11 20:48
> 声称该软件具有开源的所有好处和承诺，但实际上它并不具备，这是简单明了的欺骗行为。

来自“SSPL 不是开源许可证”<https://opensource.org/blog/the-sspl-is-not-an-open-source-l...>

> It's deception, plain and simple, to claim that the software has all the benefits and promises of open source when it does not.

From "The SSPL is Not an Open Source License" <https://opensource.org/blog/the-sspl-is-not-an-open-source-l...>

在 HN 中查看
queenkjuul 2026/04/11 16:06
我很“幸运”，没有足够聪明或足够重要来思考这个问题。无论如何，我完全同意——在这一点上，我个人可以公开发布的任何内容，要么是完全开源的，要么是完全私有的。只有当我相对确定开源不会赚到大笔钱时，我才会选择开源。
I'm "lucky" to not be smart enough or important enough to think about this. Regardless, i wholeheartedly agree -- at this point, anything i personally could release publicly, will either be fully open source, or completely private. And I'm only choosing open source if I'm relatively sure it's not gonna make some asshole tons of money.
在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/11 21:38
这就是大型企业如何战略性地使用开源的大致情况。他们试图杀死除了他们所统治的层之外的任何其他层的每个人的价值提取护城河。 因此他们将其补品商品化[0]。他们不在乎你是否基于他们的开源软件赚钱，只要你与其他也有权使用它的人竞争，并将除公司利润中心之外的任何东西变成无处不在的商品。所以他们让“混蛋”的动机与他们自己的动机一致。

[0] https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2002/06/12/strategy-letter-v/

That's in the ballpark how big corps use open source strategically. They try to kill everyone value extraction moat at any other layer than the ones they dominate. So they commoditize their complement [0]. They don't care if you make money based on their OSS, as long as you race to the bottom against anyone else who also has access to it and turn anything but the corp's profit center into a ubiquitous commodity. So they make the "asshole"'s incentives line up with their own.

[0] https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2002/06/12/strategy-letter-v/

在 HN 中查看
growrow 2026/04/11 22:31
该链接读起来很棒，并且提出了一个要点！企业投资 OSS 的另一个原因是开发他们所依赖的东西 - 特殊驱动程序等 - 并且以 OSS 维护人员收取咨询费的形式利用这一点已经取得了成功。完全同意让激励措施与他们自己保持一致。
That link was a great read and makes a strong point! Another reason corps invest in OSS is to develop something they rely on - special driver, etc - and capitalizing on that in the form of OSS maintainers charging consulting fees has been successful. Exactly in agreement with making the incentives line up with their own.
在 HN 中查看
anilgulecha 2026/04/11 18:02
我知道这里的人们有一种观点，认为不要把时间留给大型庞然大物。我会用一个故事或者一个更大的观点来反驳。

早在 2006 年 7 月，我就有了一个项目的想法，我满怀热情地建立了一个邮件列表，但最终从未实现它。这是一个非常独特的名字。

2012 年，另一位开发人员为他们的项目使用了相同的名称，但看到邮件列表已被占用，并询问他是否可以接管，我答应了，因为这是另一个人在密码学和开源领域做一些事情，这是我当时（和现在）最喜欢的两件事。

该项目名为“scrypt”，开发者是 Colin！ :) 当时我对 Colin 或 tarsnap 一无所知，IIRC。

有时，你只是做力所能及的善事，与那些让你有社区感的人一起做事，而不期望任何商业行为。因果报应会累积起来，它的好处是巨大的，尽管很难总是清楚地表达出来。

I understand people have a viewpoint here about not giving time to large behemoths. I'll counter with a story and perhaps a larger point.

Back in 2006/7 I had an idea for a project for which, in all enthusiasm, I setup a mailing list, but ended up never pursuing it. It's a very unique name.

In 2012, another developer landed on the same name for their project, but saw that the mailing list was taken up and reach out inquiring if he could take over, and I obliged because here's another person doing something in cryptography and open source, 2 of my favorite things then (and now).

The project was "scrypt" and the developer was Colin! :) I knew nothing about Colin or tarsnap then, IIRC.

Sometimes you just do kindnesses of which you're able, with people who you feel a sense of community with, without expectation of anything commercial. Karma adds up, and it's benefits are large, though hard to always articulate.

在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/04/11 19:00 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
CoryOndrejka 2026/04/11 20:31
> 事实上，在 Jeff Barr 在《第二人生》中举办的一次 AWS 用户聚会中

这句话有很多让我微笑的地方。很容易忘记，《第二人生》也是 AWS 最早的用户之一，首先是 S3。杰夫·贝佐斯 (Jeff Bezos) 亲自投资了我们 2005 年的融资（在此之前，林登实验室 (Linden Lab) 已成为独角兽），并向我们介绍了杰夫·巴尔 (Jeff Barr) 和来自 AWS 的工作。

作为回报，Jeff Barr 开始在 Second Life 中举办 AWS 聚会 - 这是许多团体建立 Second Life 前哨站的时代，从 Jonathan Coulton 到路透社。

> in fact in one of Jeff Barr's AWS user meetups in Second Life

There's so much about that phrase that makes me smile. Easy to forget that Second Life was also one of the earliest users of AWS, S3 first. Jeff Bezos had personally invested in our 2005 round (a round that made Linden Lab a unicorn before that was a thing) and pointed us at Jeff Barr and the work coming from AWS.

In return, Jeff Barr started hosting AWS meetups in Second Life -- this was the era of lots of groups setting up Second Life outposts, from Jonathan Coulton to Reuters.

在 HN 中查看
MobileVet 2026/04/11 21:18
我永远不会忘记在一次会议上第一次看到《第二人生》，我想是在弗拉格斯塔夫。 你们有一个折叠桌摊位（就像我们所有人一样）和运行《第二人生》的计算机。 我们的团队认为这很酷，后来我们在办公室讨论了很多。 那是2002年或2003年。

我们与 Evolution Robotics 一起展示了 ER1，一款新的业余爱好者机器人。

肯定有美好的回忆！

I’ll never forget seeing Second Life for the first time at a conference, in Flagstaff I think. You guys had a single folding table booth (as we all did) and computer running Second Life. Our team thought it was pretty cool and we talked about it quite a bit back at the office later. It was either 2002 or 2003.

We were with Evolution Robotics and were showing off the ER1, a new hobbyist robot.

Good memories for sure!

在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/12 01:43
事实上，当 re:Invent 2020 变得虚拟时，我们有了一个可以四处走动的虚拟空间，我有一种似曾相识的感觉——当然，笔记本电脑上的《第二人生》与佩戴 VR 护目镜有很大不同。
Indeed, when re:Invent 2020 went virtual and we had a virtual space we could walk around I had a major sense of deja vu -- of course Second Life on a laptop was very different from wearing VR goggles.
在 HN 中查看
redoh 2026/04/11 20:50
该主题中的许多“亚马逊免费劳动力”框架都忽略了这里的核心动态。 Colin 并不是在做慈善工作，他只是让 FreeBSD 在 EC2 上运行，因为 Tarsnap 确实依赖于它。这可能是开源贡献最健康的模式：您修复自己产品所在的基础设施，下游的每个人也都受益。另一种选择是等待亚马逊关心你的利基平台，这可能意味着永远等待。这与独立开发人员编写一个 AWS 封装到托管服务中的库不同。
A lot of the "free labor for Amazon" framing in this thread misses the core dynamic here. Colin wasn't doing charity work, he was making FreeBSD run on EC2 because Tarsnap literally depends on it. That's probably the healthiest model for open source contribution: you fix the infrastructure your own product sits on, and everyone downstream benefits too. The alternative is waiting for Amazon to care about your niche platform, which could mean waiting forever. It's a different calculus than, say, an indie dev writing a library that AWS wraps into a managed service.
在 HN 中查看
few 2026/04/11 16:24
> 2024 年 4 月，我向一位亚马逊人吐露，我“现在在拥有 FreeBSD/EC2 方面做得并不好”，并询问他是否可以找到一些资金来支持我的工作，其理论是在某个时刻时间和美元是可以互换的

>我通过 GitHub Sponsors 获得了 Amazon 的赞助，为期一年，每周 10 小时

不管出于什么原因，我记得我很震惊，因为你们每小时只收取 300 美元 [1]，这只是一个 L6 谷歌工程师的薪水。我希望他们现在付给你更多的钱

[1] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=30188512

> In April 2024 I confided in an Amazonian that I was "not really doing a good job of owning FreeBSD/EC2 right now" and asked if he could find some funding to support my work, on the theory that at a certain point time and dollars are fungible

>I received sponsorship from Amazon via GitHub Sponsors for 10 hours per week for a year

For whatever reason, I remember being shocked that you were only charging $300/hr [1] which was what a mere L6 google engineer would make salaried. I hope they are paying you more nowadays

[1] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=30188512

在 HN 中查看
rcbdev 2026/04/11 16:46
美国IT行业的小时工资确实很疯狂。我想知道雇用美国人的增值是否真的值得，在讲德语的欧盟，你可以以 120 欧元/小时的价格获得真正一流的工程。再往东走就更不用说了。
American hourly rates in IT are truly nuts. I wonder if the value-add to hiring American is really worth it, in German-speaking EU you'd get real top-notch engineering for 120€/h. Even less further eastwards.
在 HN 中查看
spicyusername 2026/04/11 19:23
这些薪资待遇基本上只在西海岸和纽约这个美国的“小”地区。

100-200k，是你在其他地方所期望的。这仍然相当不错，只是不是天文数字。

Those pay packages are basically only on the west coast and in New York, a "small" region of the U.S.

100-200k, is what you'd expect elsewhere. Which is still pretty good, just not astronomical.

在 HN 中查看
ignoramous 2026/04/11 19:04
> 在欧盟德语区，您只需 120 欧元/小时即可获得真正一流的工程

无意冒犯德语工程师，但科林不是 仅仅 “一流”，他是“顶尖”。

高额薪水（就像支付给“顶级”专业团队运动中的“顶级”运动员的薪水）在科技界已不再是闻所未闻。例如，谷歌支付了 20 亿美元以上，重新聘请了 c.ai 的诺姆·沙泽尔 (Noam Shazeer)。据传 Meta 支付超过 2000 万美元的薪水来挖走苏黎世的 OpenAI 研究人员。

> German-speaking EU you'd get real top-notch engineering for 120€/h

No disrespect to German-speaking engs, but Colin isn't merely "top-notch", he's "the top".

Huge salaries (like those paid to "top" athletes in "top" professional team sports) aren't unheard of in Tech anymore. For instance, Google paid $2b+ to acquihire Noam Shazeer of c.ai back. Meta was rumoured to be paying $20m+ salaries to poach OpenAI researchers based in Zurich.

在 HN 中查看
christophilus 2026/04/11 18:58
120 美元/小时在美国也能让你成为一名非常优秀的开发人员。只是不在湾区或西雅图。
$120/hr gets you a very good developer in the US, too. Just not in the Bay Area or Seattle.
在 HN 中查看
evantbyrne 2026/04/11 21:28
考虑到风险、不计费工作和增加的税率，1099 工作的现行费率往往高于此值。将开发人员借给客户的机构收取 2-3 倍的费用。请记住，工程师现在可以远程工作，这使得区域费率更加模糊。
The going rate for 1099 work tends to be higher than this to account for risk, unbillable work, and increased tax rate. Agencies that lend out their developers to clients charge 2-3x this. Remember that engineers can work remotely now which makes regional rates much fuzzier.
在 HN 中查看
loloquwowndueo 2026/04/11 21:22
他们只想获得至少与水管工相同的收入。
They just want to earn at least the same as a plumber.
在 HN 中查看
bob1029 2026/04/11 15:56
我强烈不同意关于 EC2 的 IAM 角色的部分

> 一项有用的改进（特别是考虑到 Capital One 违规后的紧迫性），但在我看来，这只是缓解一种特定的利用路径，而不是解决通过完全不适合该目的的接口暴露凭证的根本问题。

作者建议我们使用什么替代接口来安全地交换凭证？我能想到的唯一其他方法是让猴子的手直接接触秘密材料。 Outlook、slack 和团队不可能比 IMDSv2 更安全。我认为如果你手动传递 PFX 文件之类的东西，你就已经输了。

IAM 角色的全部意义在于让一切成为政策问题而不是程序问题。当你玩遍所有边缘时，这里的差异是疯狂的。 IAM 策略管理比其他路径更容易锁定。我可以在 5 分钟内向审核员证明，从数学上讲，我的团队成员甚至不可能 看 我们为某些供应商使用的签名密钥不会触发对其他管理员的警报。我有 KMS 签名密钥，但无法使用我的设备删除 根 帐户，因为我在创建时应用了不适当的策略。这个东西如果用得好的话可以非常强大。 Azure 有类似的想法，可以让访问诸如 mssql 服务器之类的东西变得不那么混乱。

I strongly disagree with the part about IAM roles for EC2

> a useful improvement (especially given the urgency after the Capital One breach) but in my view just a mitigation of one particular exploit path rather than addressing the fundamental problem that credentials were being exposed via an interface which was entirely unsuitable for that purpose.

What alternative interface does the author propose we use to securely exchange credentials? The only other approaches I can come up with involve allowing monkey hands to come into direct contact with secret materials. Outlook, slack and teams cannot possibly be more secure than IMDSv2. I think if you are manually passing around things like PFX files you've already lost the game.

The entire point of the IAM roles is to make everything a matter of policy rather than procedure. The difference here is insane when you play through all of the edges. IAM policy management is significantly easier to lock down than the alternative paths. I can prove to an auditor in 5 minutes that it is mathematically impossible for a member of my team to even see the signing keys we use for certain vendors without triggering alerts to other administrators. I've got KMS signing keys that I cannot delete with my root account because I applied inappropriate policies at creation time. This stuff can be very powerful when used well. Azure has a similar idea that makes accessing things like mssql servers way less messy.

在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/11 22:02
作者建议我们使用什么替代接口来安全地交换凭证？

如果您阅读链接的帖子，您会发现当时我建议使用 XenStore 将凭据传递给操作系统内核。 显然，Nitro 需要采取不同的方法，但如果有的话，现在会更容易。

一旦内核拥有它们，它们就可以通过合成文件系统暴露给应用程序，最重要的是，可以在其上设置所有权和权限。

我绝对不反对 EC2 的 IAM 角色。 我认为他们选择了最糟糕的接口来传输这些角色凭证。

What alternative interface does the author propose we use to securely exchange credentials?

If you read the linked post you'll see that at the time I suggested using XenStore to pass credentials to the OS kernel. Obviously a different approach would be needed with Nitro but if anything it would be easier now.

Once the kernel had them they could be exposed to applications via a synthetic filesystem which, crucially, can have ownership and permissions set on it.

I'm absolutely not arguing against IAM Roles for EC2. I'm arguing that they picked the worst possible interface over which to transmit those role credentials.

在 HN 中查看
daniel-levin 2026/04/11 23:48
在 AI 热潮之前，有计划允许访客分割 AWS 管理的 enclave，并仅将 IMDS 暴露给该 enclave [0]。客人永远不会看到任何信用信息。该飞地将充当签名预言机。在该项目以 GENAi LEADeRsHiP 的名义被搁置之前，确切的接口正在开发中。

[0] 86a7b126fea03dd57e6a3c9c9b7951b5318d33029cef0547ff441862174682f5 是搁置项目名称的 sha256

Before the AI craze, there were plans to allow guests to cleave off an AWS-managed enclave and expose IMDS to only that enclave [0]. No creds would ever be visible to the guest. The enclave would function as a signing oracle. The exact interface was being bikeshod before the project was shelved in the name of geNAi lEaDeRsHiP.

[0] 86a7b126fea03dd57e6a3c9c9b7951b5318d33029cef0547ff441862174682f5 is the sha256 of the shelved project’s name

在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/12 00:02
是的，我已经签署了 19 年的保密协议，所以我在公开谈论的事情上会尽量小心……
Right, I've got 19 years of NDAs so I try to be careful about what I talk about publicly...
在 HN 中查看
erincandescent 2026/04/11 18:35
Scaleway 的等效项仅允许来自 <1024 端口的连接。这很可爱，意味着只有具有 CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE 的进程才能检索令牌。

您可以对 vsock(7) 套接字执行类似的操作。这还有一个优点，即更难欺骗应用程序与 vsock 套接字建立连接。

这两种方法都有一个弱点，即为进程提供 CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE 以便它们可以侦听“特权”套接字，但它们并非完全不典型，但如果没有它，它们就会对任何东西起作用。

更好的是，您可以将引导凭据放入 DMI 数据或类似数据中，最终（在 Linux 上）位于 sysfs 目录中，该目录只能由 root 读取。

Scaleway's equivalent only allows connections from ports <1024. This is cute and means only processes with CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE can retrieve the tokens.

You can do similar with vsock(7) sockets. This also has the advantage that it's harder to trick an application into making a connection to a vsock socket.

Both of these have the weakness that it is not entirely atypical to give processes CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE so they can listen on "privileged" sockets, but they work against anything without that.

Even better, you could put bootstrap credentials in DMI data or similar, where it'll end up (on Linux) inside a sysfs directory which can only be read by root.

在 HN 中查看
arjie 2026/04/11 17:52
精彩的传说。阅读旅程令人着迷。但也在这里听到了一些名字（例如，塔维斯·奥曼迪因其在零号计划中的角色而闻名），并且知道即使是顶级工程师也会因为做出错误的选择而轰炸采访。

除了我喜欢那些实际上做了很多事情的人的这些博客文章之外，没有什么有用的补充。过去的美好总结。

Fantastic piece of lore. Fascinating to read the journey. But also hearing some of the names here (Tavis Ormandy is famous for his role on Project Zero, for instance) and knowing that even top engineers can bomb interviews for making poor choices.

Nothing useful to add except that I Like these blog posts from someone who actually did a bunch of things. Nice round-up of the past.

在 HN 中查看
electroly 2026/04/12 02:30
我忘记了您必须尽早单独请求 AWS 服务。我检查了 2007 年以来的电子邮件历史记录，确实如此，我最初是 仅有的 获得“亚马逊电子商务服务”访问权限！我收到了一封单独的电子邮件，确认我已注册 S3。有趣的是，他们还没有意识到自动“套餐交易”是他们最大的卖点之一。

我注册的下一个服务是“Alexa Web Information Service”。网络搜索即服务，当时“Alexa”是他们收购的搜索公司，而不是语音助手。到 2007 年中期，我（最终）被接受加入 EC2 beta。正如他们所说，其余的都是历史了。

I had forgotten that you had to individually request AWS services early on. I checked my email history from 2007 and it's true, I was initially only granted access to "Amazon E-Commerce Service"! I got a separate email confirming that I had signed up for S3. Funny that they hadn't yet figured out that the automatic "package deal" is one of their biggest selling points.

The next service I signed up for was "Alexa Web Information Service". Web search as a service, back when "Alexa" was the search company they had acquired, not a voice assistant. By mid-2007 I was (finally) accepted into the EC2 beta. The rest, as they say, is history.

在 HN 中查看
[deleted] 2026/04/12 00:41 deleted

[已删除]

[deleted]

在 HN 中查看
MyUltiDev 2026/04/12 00:22
20 年来的回顾，看不到 Hetzner 或 OVH 的数据，就足以说明问题了。我在 AWS、Hetzner 和几个较小的提供商之间运行工作负载，差距并不微妙。对于中小型 Web 堆栈，您在 AWS 上的价格约为 350 美元，而在 Hetzner 上类似规格的价格为 20 到 25 欧元，再加上 20 TB 带宽，而不是在前 100 个之后按每场 9 美分计费。AWS 目前真正销售的不是计算，而是 IAM 模型、全球覆盖范围、深度集成以及组织结构图共识，即没有人会因为选择它而被解雇。这是一个真正的产品，在一些商店中价值很高，但它与 2006 年云的含义截然不同。对于最近真正将实际工作负载从 AWS 上移走的人来说，哪部分比您预期的更痛苦？
A 20 year retrospective with no Hetzner or OVH numbers in sight is a bit of a tell. I run workloads across AWS, Hetzner, and a couple of smaller providers, and the gap is not subtle. For a small to medium web stack you are looking at roughly $350 a month on AWS versus 20 to 25 euros on Hetzner for similar specs, plus 20 TB of bandwidth included instead of being billed at 9 cents a gig after the first 100. What AWS actually sells at this point is not compute, it is the IAM model, the global footprint, the deep integrations, and the org chart consensus that nobody gets fired for picking it. That is a real product and worth a lot in some shops, but it is a very different product from what cloud meant in 2006. For the people who have actually moved a real workload off AWS recently, what was the part that turned out to be more painful than you expected?
在 HN 中查看
ysleepy 2026/04/11 15:28
当我经常运行 FreeBSD 并订阅邮件列表时，我记得许多这样的事件。

你到底为什么要给这个怪物般的公司提供这么多免费劳动力？

我知道志愿服务很有趣，但将你的时间和能力贡献给一家超级资本主义公司是短视的。我希望有适当的补偿，并且我不包括“抢先体验”。

I remember many of these events as I was running FreeBSD a lot and subscribed to the mailing lists.

Why on earth would you give this monstrosity of a company so much free labour?

I get that volunteering is fun, but donating your time and competence to a hyper capitalist company is short sighted. I hope there was appropriate compensation, and I'm not including "early access".

在 HN 中查看
dchest 2026/04/11 16:08
Colin，如果我没记错的话，您首先在 Ubuntu 上运行 Tarsnap 服务器，然后才让 FreeBSD 在 EC2 上运行。您什么时候有足够的信心转向 FreeBSD？
Colin, if I remember correctly, you first ran Tarsnap servers on Ubuntu before you made FreeBSD work on EC2. At what point were you confident enough to switch to FreeBSD?
在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/12 01:46
我认为 cc1.4xlarge 是我运行 Tarsnap 服务的第一个实例。 HVM 让我更加自信——PV 极大地增加了 VM/分页错误的风险，而这正是导致静默数据损坏的方式。

所以...我必须检查我的笔记才能确定，但​​我认为是 2011 年秋季？

I think cc1.4xlarge was the first instance I ran the Tarsnap service on. Being HVM made me far more confident -- PV dramatically increases the risk of VM/paging bugs, which is exactly the way to get silent data corruption.

So... I'd have to check my notes to be sure but I think fall 2011?

在 HN 中查看
wahnfrieden 2026/04/11 14:18
他给了他们那么多免费劳动力
He gave them so much free labor
在 HN 中查看
iceman28 2026/04/11 15:23
那是一个不同的时代，软件被视为是一起构建的东西，每个人都有兴趣互相学习最好的东西。
It was a different time when software was seen as something that was built together and everyone was interested in learning the best from one another.
在 HN 中查看
surgical_fire 2026/04/11 16:45
不，确实不是。他的故事发生在 2000 年代中期，而不是 1980 年代中期。

众所周知，在 2000 年代中期，这些公司的运营规模已经达到数十亿美元，他们的工程师也已经获得了丰厚的报酬。

天啊，《破解编程面试》是 2008 年出版的。当时在这些公司找到一份工作已经是令人垂涎的事情了，因为他们的薪水很高。

No, it was really not. His tale is from mid-2000s, not from mid-1980s.

In mid 2000s these companies were already operating in the billions and their engineers were already well compensated, and it was known.

Hell, "Cracking the Coding Interview" came out in 2008. Getting a job at those companies at the time was already something coveted because of how well they paid.

在 HN 中查看
aleph_minus_one 2026/04/11 19:05
> 众所周知，在 2000 年代中期，这些公司的运营规模已经达到数十亿美元，他们的工程师也已经获得了丰厚的报酬。

也许在美国，但在许多其他国家，这肯定不成立。

> In mid 2000s these companies were already operating in the billions and their engineers were already well compensated, and it was known.

Perhaps in the USA, but in many other countries this does for sure not hold.

在 HN 中查看
RyJones 2026/04/11 18:23
他想要宣传、传播和支持 FreeBSD 的采用；这种免费劳动力对此有所帮助。
He wants to evangelize, spread, and support FreeBSD adoption; this free labor helps in this.
在 HN 中查看
johng 2026/04/11 14:29
这……他们真的欠他一些东西，恕我直言。天哪，打折服务让他可以在 Tarsnap 上获得更高的利润，这对我来说听起来不错！
This... they really owe him something, IMHO. Hell, discounted service so he can make a better margin on Tarsnap sounds good to me!
在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/12 01:51
我确实有一些“免费”AWS 账户！ 但它们是用于 FreeBSD 的东西（开发、发布官方镜像以及 Amazon 托管的 FreeBSD 基础设施）——我永远不会将它们用于我的业务。

我确信我可以逃脱惩罚，亚马逊的任何人都不会注意到，但这就是事情的原则。

I do have a few "free" AWS accounts! But they're for FreeBSD stuff (development, publishing official images, and FreeBSD infrastructure which Amazon hosts) -- I would never use them for my business.

I'm sure I could get away with it and nobody at Amazon would even notice, but it's the principle of the thing.

在 HN 中查看
lnz_me 2026/04/11 19:26
Netflix 是 FreeBSD 的大用户，也是 AWS 的大用户，他们在 AWS 上运行 FreeBSD 吗？对我来说显然是赞助商，因为他们严重依赖像科林这样的志愿者建立的基础设施
Netflix is a big FreeBSD user and a big AWS user, do they run FreeBSD on AWS? Would be the obvious sponsor to me as they rely heavily on the infrastructure built by volunteers like Colin
在 HN 中查看
tiffanyh 2026/04/11 21:05
我不认为他们这样做。

Netflix 专门将 FreeBSD 用于其定制的 CDN/流媒体服务器，这些服务器直接由 ISP 托管……而不是在 AWS 上。然而，他们面向用户的目录应用程序在 AWS 托管的 Ubuntu 服务器上运行。

至少这是我在 HN 上读到的内容。

I don’t think they do.

Netflix uses FreeBSD specifically for their custom-built CDN/streaming servers, which are hosted directly with ISPs … not on AWS. Their user-facing catalog app, however, runs on Ubuntu servers hosted on AWS.

At least that’s what I recall reading here on HN.

在 HN 中查看
cperciva 2026/04/12 01:53
是的，这是正确的。 他们不是白痴，并意识到在 EC2 中启动 FreeBSD 实例对于开发目的非常有用 - 最大的 EC2 实例可以非常非常快地运行构建世界 - 但他们不需要 FreeBSD/EC2 来处理其生产工作负载。
Yes, that's correct. They're not idiots and realize that spinning up FreeBSD instances in EC2 can be very useful for development purposes -- the largest EC2 instances can run a buildworld very very fast -- but they have no need for FreeBSD/EC2 for their production workloads.
在 HN 中查看
latentframe 2026/04/11 15:31
有趣的是，这段历史是关于边缘案例和不太可能变成真实事件的风险的。系统的扩展速度比我们对其安全性的想象要快。
Interesting how this history is about the edge cases and the unlikely risks that turn into real incidents. the systems scale faster than what we think about their safety.
在 HN 中查看
mlhpdx 2026/04/11 14:20
我挖出了不久前（几年前）2006 年的原始 AWS 账户确认电子邮件。现在我需要再去找一遍，看看我是否早到了。
I dug up my original AWS account confirmation email from 2006 a while (years) back. Now I need to go find it again to see if I was earlier.
在 HN 中查看
daemonologist 2026/04/11 16:12
好域名。
Good domain name.
在 HN 中查看
cmiles8 2026/04/11 22:43
多年来，AWS 一直是无可争议的领导者，但现在感觉它已经迷失了方向。

它知道如何成为市场领导者并率先推出大型产品。现在，它正在努力应对一个在越来越多的领域落后的世界。 GenAI 早期的重大失误似乎加速了这一趋势。

早年的大量动力让它继续前进，但这个剧本只能持续这么长时间。

AWS was the clear undisputed leader for years, but feels like it’s lost its way now.

It knew how to be the market leader and first to market with big launches. It’s now struggling to navigate a world where in more and more areas it’s falling behind. The big early misses on GenAI seem to have accelerated that.

A ton of momentum from earlier years keeps it moving, but that playbook only lasts so long.

在 HN 中查看
andrewstuart 2026/04/11 18:14
我是 AWS 的早期采用者和忠实粉丝。

在某个阶段，我意识到 AWS 极其昂贵、极其缓慢、极其复杂，而且对开源抱有一种寄生态度。

我意识到我应该在其他平台上的虚拟机上全力支持 Linux。

我已经完成了。

I was an early adopter and huge fanboy for AWS.

At some stage I realised AWS is extremely expensive, extremely slow, extremely ridiculously complex and also a parasitic attitude to open source.

I realised I should instead go all in on Linux on virtual machines on other platforms.

AWS I’m done.

在 HN 中查看
villgax 2026/04/11 14:42
大约 2 年后推出的经过验证的 EC2 实例是一个很好的笑话，哈哈
That attested EC2 instance rollout after ~2 decades was a nice joke LOL
在 HN 中查看
guardiangod 2026/04/11 14:24
我只是想将 AWS 上的这篇文章与 Azure 上的文章进行对比 - https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47616242。

两家公司拥有功能相似的产品，但行为却完全不同。一家公司以安全为根本原则进行技术决策，而另一家公司则不考虑安全性。

I just want to contrast this article on AWS to its Azure counterpart- https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47616242.

2 companies have functionally similar products, but behaves completely different. One company makes technical decisions with security as the fundamental principal, while for the other company, security is not a consideration.

在 HN 中查看
jiggawatts 2026/04/11 14:35
这是一种不公平的描述！

Azure 工程师绝对考虑了安全性。

他们只是选择了其他优先事项：不惜一切代价实现增长以赶上 AWS。

That’s an unfair characterisation!

Azure engineers absolutely considered security.

They just chose other priorities: growth at any cost to catch up with AWS.

20 years of giving love to a soulless corporation
